title: Stelmark Eyes Galveston Expansion Even as Labor Fight Drags On
body:
Despite the unresolved dispute over its federal grant-backed automation contract at the Port of Galveston, Stelmark Freight has quietly begun preliminary engineering work on a second phase that would extend automated crane coverage to the port's eastern container berths, according to two people with direct knowledge of the plans.

The move has irritated ILA representatives, who said it signals bad faith during ongoing mediation. "They're pouring concrete with one hand and shaking ours with the other," said Clyde Okafor, the union's Gulf Coast director.

Stelmark declined to confirm or deny the engineering work. COO Miriam Solano said only that the company "routinely conducts planning work across its active project sites."

Port authority chair Bernard Tran said he was unaware of Stelmark's phase-two planning and that any additional work would require a separate commission vote.

The final mediation session is scheduled for March 18. If talks collapse, the ILA has said it will escalate to picketing and potentially coordinate with other Gulf port locals. The union's national office has been monitoring the situation, labor observers say.

Renata Solis, the lobbyist who has supported Stelmark's federal positioning, published an op-ed this week in a trade journal arguing that "labor agreements cannot be permitted to nullify the productivity commitments that justify public infrastructure investment." The ILA called the piece "a blueprint for union-busting with taxpayer money."
